title: Knowledge Nugget | India’s new Solid Waste Management Rules 2026: 10 key points for your UPSC exam
explain: India is updating how it handles trash! The new 2026 rules focus on a 'circular economy'—which is just a fancy way of saying we should reuse things instead of throwing them away. It emphasizes sorting waste correctly and setting goals to use more recycled plastics to protect the planet.

title: New Solid Waste Management Rules Notified; To Come into Force from April 1, 2026
explain: Starting April 1, 2026, new rules will help keep both cities and rural areas clean. One cool part is the introduction of 'Refuse Derived Fuel' (RDF). This is basically taking non-recyclable stuff like certain plastics and old clothes, shredding them, and turning them into fuel for energy!

title: Methods Involved in Plastic Waste Management
explain: There are three main ways we deal with plastic: recycling (making something new), incineration (burning it for energy), and landfills (burying it). Each has a trade-off, but recycling is the superstar because it turns old waste into brand new products!

title: Solid Waste Management (SWM) Rules, 2026 to come into effect from 1st April, 2026 | Current Affairs | Vision IAS
explain: The 2026 rules are replacing the older 2016 versions to make them better. A big change is 'Four-Stream Segregation.' This means instead of just one bin, we'll sort waste into four categories, including 'wet waste' (like food scraps) and 'dry waste' (like plastic and metal).

title: Toolkit: Plastic Waste Management - Swachh Bharat Mission
explain: To clean up India, the Swachh Bharat Mission suggests a detailed plan: figure out how much plastic is being used in homes and schools, find people to collect it door-to-door, and set up special village sheds to store the plastic before it gets processed.

title: Plastic Waste Management | Plastics and the Environment Series – Geneva Environment Network
explain: Plastic pollution is a global problem, not just a local one! Countries around the world have joined together through the 'Basel Convention' to create international guidelines on how to manage plastic and electronic waste safely so they don't harm the environment.

title: THE PLASTIC WASTE MANAGEMENT RULES, 2016 (as amended to date)
explain: These rules explain that local city governments are the ones responsible for the 'heavy lifting.' They have to build the systems to collect, store, and move plastic waste to the right places so it doesn't end up littering our streets.
